creator: ota@j.email.ne.jp
text:
It will fix the problem although it will be extracted to the same directory when revision is increased.  It will overwrite the old build and then apply new patches, run configure, compile, and so on.


The port system creates 'COOKIES' for some important steps.  For example, if .build_done cookie is there, ports considers build is completed.  In the current system, it only contains port-name.  Therefore, it doesn't know if if version or revision is increased.

You cannot depend on clean up.  For example, A run-depends on B, B run-depends on C, C run-depends on D, D run-depends on E.  Because all of these are run-depends, the port system builds A first and just before A is installed, it finds that B has to be installed before A.  Let's say port E does not compile.

In this case, you cannot install nor clean any of them.  Then, you find that port E is fixed and do csup/cvsup.  Port B revision was also updated but you didn't notice.  Then, when you install port A, it installs the old port B with new revision number.  If the dependecy-chain is very long like KDE or specially GNOME, you could easily miss some updates in the middle.

Regards,
Hiro

On Fri, 23 Mar 2007 09:33:40 +0100
Pav Lucistnik <pav@FreeBSD.org> wrote:

> I'm not sure this solves the problem. When portrevision increases, but
> ${WRKSRC} does not change, the second build will still hit the files
> left from the first one, no?
> 
> You should really clean up after every build.
> 
> -- 
> Pav Lucistnik <pav@oook.cz>
>               <pav@FreeBSD.org>
> 
> Two sausages are in a frying pan. One says, "Geez, it's hot in here isn't it?"
> And the other one says, "Aaaaaah! A talking sausage!"
